sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, LEFT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 69, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 68 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the ascii output, I can see the container structure more clearly now:
Left chamber (yellow background):
- Rows 15-18: all B (black walls)
- Rows 19-23: BBBBBBBBBSSSSSYY (left square at cols 19-23)
- Rows 24-25: BBBBBBBBBYYYYYYY (empty, yellow)
Right chamber (orange background):
- Rows 35-38: OOOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBO (orange with black walls)
- Rows 39-43: OOOOOOOOOSSSSSBBBBBO (right square at cols 39-43)
- Rows 44-50: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBO (all black walls)
So the chambers have specific shapes. The left square is in a yellow region, the right square is in an orange region. The container walls are black.
I notice the chambers have different shapes. The left chamber seems to have a vertical corridor, while the right chamber has a more complex shape.
Let me think about what the goal might be. Given that:
1. There are two squares that move in a coupled way
2. The chambers have specific shapes
3. There are no obvious target markers
Maybe the goal is to position the squares so they fill specific areas, or maybe there's a pattern to match.
Let me look at the full container boundary to understand the chamber shapes better. The container boundary from the segmentation is:
[[9, 19], [9, 48], [13, 48], [14, 49], [14, 53], [53, 53], [53, 49], [54, 48], [58, 48], [58, 34], [44, 34], [44, 43], [43, 44], [39, 44], [39, 48], [38, 49], [29, 49], [28, 48], [28, 44], [24, 44], [23, 43], [23, 39], [19, 39], [18, 38], [18, 34], [14, 34], [13, 33], [13, 29], [14, 28], [18, 28], [18, 24], [19, 23], [23, 23], [23, 19], [24, 18], [38, 18], [39, 19], [39, 23], [43, 23], [44, 24], [44, 28], [58, 28], [58, 9], [39, 9], [39, 13], [38, 14], [29, 14], [28, 13], [28, 9], [19, 9], [19, 13], [18, 14], [14, 14], [14, 18], [13, 19]]
This is a complex shape. Let me try to visualize it:
- The container spans from row 9 to row 58, and from col 9 to col 53.
- The left chamber is roughly cols 9-28, rows 14-38
- The right chamber is roughly cols 34-53, rows 14-53
Wait, I see there's a middle section too. Let me look at the ascii more carefully.
Actually, let me focus on finding the goal. Maybe I should try to move the squares to specific positions and see if anything happens (like a level completion).
Let me try a different approach: maybe the goal is to move both squares to their "home" positions, which might be the corners of their chambers. Let me try moving the left square to the bottom-left corner and the right square to the bottom-right corner.
Current positions:
- Left: (19, 19)
- Right: (39, 44)
To move left square down, I need DOWN. To move it left, I need LEFT.
But LEFT also moves the right square right, and DOWN moves both squares down.
Let me think about this more carefully. The movement is:
- UP: both squares move up (row -5)
- DOWN: both squares move down (row +5)
- LEFT: left square col -5, right square col +5
- RIGHT: left square col +5, right square col -5
So to move the left square to (row, col) = (max_row, 9) and the right square to (max_row, 49), I need to:
1. Move both down as much as possible
2. Move left as much as possible (left square to col 9, right square to col 49)
But the squares have different vertical bounds. The left square can go from row 19 to some max, and the right square can go from row 39 to some max.
Let me try moving both down and see what happens. If the left square hits its bottom bound first, it will stop while the right square continues.
Actually, I realize I should first understand the full chamber layout. Let me look at more of the ascii to see the full container shape.
Let me try a different approach: move the squares to extreme positions and see if the level completes. I'll try:
1. Move both squares as far down as possible
2. Move them as far apart as possible (LEFT)
3. Check if level completes
If not, I'll try other combinations.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (19,19), right at (39,44)
# Let me map the full vertical range for each square by moving down repeatedly

# Move down several times to find bottom bounds
for i in range(6):
    action(['DOWN'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    left_pos = None
    right_pos = None
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
                left_pos = (n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1])
            else:
                right_pos = (n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1])
    changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
    print(f"DOWN {i+1}: left={left_pos}, right={right_pos}, changed={changed}")
    if not changed:
        print("  No more movement possible DOWN")
        break

# Now try LEFT to find left bounds
for i in range(4):
    action(['LEFT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    left_pos = None
    right_pos = None
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
                left_pos = (n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1])
            else:
                right_pos = (n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1])
    changed = last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)
    print(f"LEFT {i+1}: left={left_pos}, right={right_pos}, changed={changed}")
    if not changed:
        print("  No more movement possible LEFT")
        break

print(f"\nFinal: left={left_pos}, right={right_pos}")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed', False))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5", "UP": "Both squares row-5 if not blocked", "DOWN": "Both squares row+5 if not blocked"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber background", "orange": "Right chamber background"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left square: row 19(top)-?, col 9-19. Right square: row 39(top)-?, col 39-49. Coupled movement.", "goal_model": "Unknown - testing extreme positions for level completion.", "recent_findings": ["Left chamber has yellow background, right has orange", "Container is complex shape with two chambers"], "open_questions": ["What is the target configuration?", "What are bottom bounds for each square?"], "current_plan": "Move squares to extreme positions, check for level completion.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

DOWN 2: left=(19, 19), right=(49, 44), changed=True
DOWN 3: left=(19, 19), right=(54, 44), changed=True
DOWN 4: left=(19, 19), right=(54, 44), changed=True
DOWN 5: left=(19, 19), right=(54, 44), changed=False
  No more movement possible DOWN
LEFT 1: left=(19, 14), right=(54, 44), changed=True
LEFT 2: left=(19, 9), right=(54, 44), changed=True
LEFT 3: left=(19, 9), right=(54, 44), changed=False

Final: left=(19, 9), right=(54, 44)
